comment_text.sn23.1:2.1: For Māra’s mythic history, see SN 4.1:2.1. Early Buddhist texts present Māra as both a deity of ill intent, and as a metaphor for the death, desire, and entrapment in the world of transmigration. This discourse highlights the literal meaning of his name: Death.
comment_text.sn23.1:2.3: Here we could translate Māra as “murder”.
comment_text.sn23.1:3.11: Ogādha (see gādha at SN 22.56:4.2) is the “firm footing”, reaching which is the “objective” of fording a river. It inhabits the same metaphorical sphere as the “far shore” (pāra) which one safely reaches (MN 34:9.2), and about which the final chapter of the Sutta Nipāta was sung (Snp 5.18:1.4), or the “solid ground” on which the brahmin stands (SN 35.228:2.10). The “far shore”, meanwhile, may also be the parāyana or “destination”.

root_text.sn23.1:1.2: Atha kho āyasmā rādho yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ abhivādetvā ekamantaṁ nisīdi. Ekamantaṁ nisinno kho āyasmā rādho bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn23.1:2.3: “Rūpe kho, rādha, sati māro vā assa māretā vā yo vā pana mīyati.
root_text.sn23.1:2.4: Tasmātiha tvaṁ, rādha, rūpaṁ māroti passa, māretāti passa, mīyatīti passa, rogoti passa, gaṇḍoti passa, sallanti passa, aghanti passa, aghabhūtanti passa.
root_text.sn23.1:2.5: Ye naṁ evaṁ passanti te sammā passanti.
root_text.sn23.1:2.9: viññāṇe sati māro vā assa māretā vā yo vā pana mīyati.
root_text.sn23.1:2.10: Tasmātiha tvaṁ, rādha, viññāṇaṁ māroti passa, māretāti passa, mīyatīti passa, rogoti passa, gaṇḍoti passa, sallanti passa, aghanti passa, aghabhūtanti passa.
root_text.sn23.1:2.11: Ye naṁ evaṁ passanti, te sammā passantī”ti.
root_text.sn23.1:3.10: “Accayāsi, rādha, pañhaṁ, nāsakkhi pañhassa pariyantaṁ gahetuṁ.
root_text.sn23.1:3.11: Nibbānogadhañhi, rādha, brahmacariyaṁ vussati, nibbānaparāyanaṁ nibbānapariyosānan”ti.

translation_text.sn23.1:1.2: Then Venerable Rādha went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him:
translation_text.sn23.1:2.1: “Sir, they speak of this thing called ‘Māra’.
translation_text.sn23.1:2.3: “When there is form, Rādha, there would be Māra, or the murderer, or the murdered.
translation_text.sn23.1:2.4: So you should see form as Māra, the murderer, the murdered, the diseased, the boil, the dart, the gloom, the gloominess.
translation_text.sn23.1:2.5: Those who see it like this see rightly.
translation_text.sn23.1:2.9: consciousness, there may be Māra, or the murderer, or the murdered.
translation_text.sn23.1:2.10: So you should see consciousness as Māra, the murderer, the murdered, the diseased, the boil, the dart, the gloom, the gloominess.
translation_text.sn23.1:2.11: Those who see it like this see rightly.”
translation_text.sn23.1:3.1: “But sir, what’s the purpose of seeing rightly?”
translation_text.sn23.1:3.2: “Disillusionment is the purpose of seeing rightly.”
translation_text.sn23.1:3.9: “But sir, what is the purpose of extinguishment?”
translation_text.sn23.1:3.10: “Your question goes too far, Rādha. You couldn’t figure out the limit of questions.
translation_text.sn23.1:3.11: For extinguishment is the objective, destination, and culmination of the spiritual life.”

variant_text.sn23.1:0.2: Paṭhamamāravagga → paṭhamavaggo (sya-all); paṭhamo vaggo (pts1ed); paṭhamavagga (csp1ed, csp2ed)
variant_text.sn23.1:3.10: Accayāsi → accasarā (bj, sya-all, km); assa (pts1ed); accayā (mr)
